sanskrit_devanagari:
स्वधर्ममपि चावेक्ष्य न विकम्पितुमर्हसि ।
धर्म्याद्धि युद्धाच्छ्रेयोऽन्यत्क्षत्रियस्य न विद्यते ॥ ३१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sva-dharmam api cāvekṣya na vikampitum arhasi |
dharmyād dhi yuddhāc chreyo 'nyat kṣatriyasya na vidyate || 31 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs in source pack)
exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)

exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.031 = MBh_06,024.031). Word-segmentation differs: GRETIL writes 'svadharmam' as one word; modern editions write 'sva-dharmam' with hyphen. Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: one's own prescribed duty / duty proper to one's role
to waver / to hesitate / to tremble
of a kṣatriya / of the warrior order

translations.verbatim_quote: Considering your specific duty as a kṣatriya, you should know that there is no better engagement for you than fighting on religious principles; and so there is no need for hesitation.
Besides, considering your duty as a warrior, you should not waver. Indeed, for a warrior, there is no better engagement than fighting for upholding of righteousness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Out of the four orders of social administration, the second order, for the matter of good administration, is called kṣatriya. Kṣat means hurt. One who gives protection from harm is called kṣatriya (trāyate – to give protection). The kṣatriyas are trained for killing in the forest.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.31 Api, even; aveksya, considering; svadharmam, your own duty, the duty of a Ksatriya, viz battle — considering even that — ; na arhasi, you ought not; vikampitum, to waver, to deviate from the natural duty of the Ksatriya, i.e. from what is natural to yourself.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads svadharmam as 'the natural duty of the Kṣatriya' — the duty proper to one's specific station, not duty in the abstract. The 'api' ('even') is doing real work: even leaving aside the metaphysical argument of 2.11-2.30, the kṣatriya-duty argument alone forbids hesitation. The 'hi' (since) of the second hemistich grounds the claim: a battle 'not devoid of righteousness' (na dharma-rahita) but 'supremely righteous' is the highest good available to a kṣatriya, because it is conducive to virtue and protects the governed.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use; bhagavad-gita.us serves the same English text under both Shankara and Ramanuja headings for this verse, presumably a sourcing error in the aggregator. Substantive Ramanuja position recorded in summary_paraphrase below from Adidevananda Vol. 1.)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.31 Api, even; aveksya, considering; svadharmam, your own duty, the duty of a Ksatriya, viz battle — considering even that — ; na arhasi, you ought not; vikampitum, to waver. (Note: the bhagavad-gita.us page serves identical text under both commentary headings for 2.31; flagged in source-pack disagreements.)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads svadharma as the action prescribed for one's specific varṇa-and-āśrama position by Vedic injunction — kṣatriya battle in this case. The 'api' is concessive: even granting Arjuna's emotional resistance, the prescribed duty argument stands independently. For Ramanuja, prescribed action performed without fruit-attachment becomes worship of the Lord, so abandoning prescribed action does not buy spiritual freedom; it forfeits the actual path. (Sridhara of Rudra Sampradaya and Vishvanatha of Gaudiya Sampradaya echo this on the same page — multi-Sampradaya convergence on the verse's basic claim is high.)

positions.rendering: Considering your specific duty as a kṣatriya
Besides, considering your duty as a warrior
Even — api — when one considers svadharma alone, separately from the imperishable-Self argument of 2.11-2.30, the conclusion 'do not waver' follows
Even — api — granting your emotional resistance, the Veda-prescribed duty for a kṣatriya is battle; that prescription is independent of how you feel
'Besides' is the explicit rendering of api — naming this verse as a second, independent argument added to the first

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All five sources agree that svadharma is role-specific (the kṣatriya's duty, not duty in general) and that 'api' marks this as an independent argument. They disagree on the rhetorical scope of api. Shankara reads it as 'even apart from the imperishable doctrine just given.' Ramanuja reads it as 'even granting Arjuna's resistance.' Mukundananda flattens it to 'besides.' The substantive doctrinal claim — that svadharma is varṇa-specific and that for a kṣatriya it is dharma-yuddha — is uncontested across Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, Gaudiya, Rudra, and Kumāra Sampradāyas. A separate sourcing issue: bhagavad-gita.us serves identical English under both Shankara and Ramanuja for 2.31 (likely a CMS error); the substantive Ramanuja position in his Gītā-bhāṣya does diverge from Shankara on adhikāra, even though for this single verse the rendering aligns. Flagged for transparency rather than papering over.

literal_meaning: Also considering your own duty (svadharma), you ought not to waver; for a kṣatriya, there is no greater good than a battle in accord with dharma.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots from the imperishable-Self argument (2.11-2.30) to a second, independent argument: even setting metaphysics aside, role-specific duty (svadharma) forbids Arjuna's withdrawal. The 'api' marks the verse as an additional argument, not a restatement. The verse names two doctrinal moves: (a) duty is role-specific — what binds Arjuna binds him because he is a kṣatriya, not because he is a person; (b) for a kṣatriya, a battle that conforms to dharma (dharma-yuddha) is the highest available good, because it protects the governed and conforms to varṇa-prescribed action. Shankara, Ramanuja, Sridhara, Vishvanatha, Keshava Kashmiri, and Abhinavagupta all converge on this reading; the verse is doctrinally non-controversial across Sampradāyas. The verse opens the karma-yoga block (2.31-2.53) of the chapter: where 2.11-2.30 said 'what survives,' 2.31 begins 'what to do.'

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — verse text identical to Shankara on this aggregator; flagged)
remaining_gaps: bhagavad-gita.us serves the same English under both Shankara and Ramanuja headings for 2.31; cross-checking Ramanuja's actual Sanskrit/English Gita-bhāṣya under Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math) for this verse is logged as a follow-up, but the substantive Ramanuja position is recorded honestly in summary_paraphrase rather than fabricated.
